note.soap:
S:
Patient reports tick bite. Reports a tick bite with a bullseye rash present. No systemic symptoms reported. Exposure occurred in Georgia.
O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Patient describes characteristic erythema migrans rash consistent with bullseye appearance.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease, given the bullseye rash and tick exposure in an endemic area.  
  - Differential:  
    1. Early Lyme disease  
    2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
    3. Cellulitis or other local skin infection  
    4. Allergic reaction to tick bite  
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, neurologic signs, or joint pain reported. No signs of severe allergic reaction.

P:  
  - Disposition: Routine follow-up with primary care or infectious disease provider recommended.  
  - Plan: Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy for early Lyme disease per guidelines (e.g., doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-21 days) if no contraindications. Educate patient on signs of progression including fever, joint pain, neurologic symptoms.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever, worsening rash, joint swelling, neurologic symptoms, or other systemic signs develop. Return for in-person evaluation if symptoms worsen or do not improve within 72 hours of treatment initiation.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- CDC guidance for Lyme disease emphasizes that erythema migrans rash with tick exposure in endemic areas is diagnostic and warrants treatment without waiting for serology.  
- AAFP review supports empiric doxycycline treatment for early Lyme disease presenting with characteristic rash and no contraindications.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
